title: Modules, Packages & Import System
description: Python's import system from basics to Staff+ depth: module caching, relative vs absolute imports, namespace packages, lazy loading, and large-scale package architecture.

sections.title: 1. How Python Finds and Loads Modules
sections.description:
When import foo runs: (1) Check sys.modules cache. (2) Find the module via sys.meta_path finders (Finder -> Loader). (3) Execute the module code (fills the module's namespace). (4) Add the module to sys.modules. (5) Bind foo in the caller's namespace.

sys.path controls where finders look. It's initialized from PYTHONPATH, virtualenv site-packages, and the current directory. Modifying it at runtime is a code smell.

FAANG angle: 'Why does import foo run the module code only once?' — caching in sys.modules. Subsequent imports get the cached module object without re-executing.
sections.codeBlock:
import sys

# sys.modules: cache of loaded modules
print('math' in sys.modules)  # True (stdlib pre-loaded)
import math
print(id(math) == id(sys.modules['math']))  # True

# sys.path: module search order
for p in sys.path[:3]:
    print(p)  # current dir, PYTHONPATH, site-packages

# Reload a module (rarely needed, useful in REPL)
import importlib
importlib.reload(math)  # re-executes module code

# Finding a module's location
print(math.__file__)  # '/usr/lib/python3.12/math.cpython-312-darwin.so'

# Check if module is built-in
import marshal
print(marshal.__name__)  # marshal
print(hasattr(marshal, '__file__'))  # False (built-in)

sections.title: 2. Absolute vs Relative Imports — The Right Choice
sections.description:
Absolute imports (from package.submodule import X): explicit, unambiguous, refactor-proof. Use them by default.

Relative imports (from .submodule import X): implicit, break on reorganization. Only use when the package name is unwieldy or for deep internal references. NEVER use from .. more than two levels deep — it's a sign to reorganize.

__init__.py controls what from package import * exports (__all__) and can aggregate submodule names for convenience.

FAANG angle: 'Why does from . import sibling fail when running a script directly?' — __package__ is not set for __main__. Use python -m package.module instead of python module.py.
sections.codeBlock:
# === package structure ===
# myapp/
#   __init__.py
#   utils/
#     __init__.py
#     strings.py
#   models/
#     __init__.py
#     user.py
#   tests/
#     __init__.py
#     test_user.py

# Absolute import (preferred)
# myapp/models/user.py
from myapp.utils.strings import slugify

# Relative import (acceptable for sibling modules)
# myapp/tests/test_user.py
from ..models.user import User

# __init__.py aggregation
# myapp/__init__.py
from .models.user import User
from .utils.strings import slugify
# Now: from myapp import User, slugify

# __all__ controls * exports
# myapp/models/__init__.py
__all__ = ['User', 'Profile']

# Avoid: circular imports
# Use TYPE_CHECKING guard for type annotations
from __future__ import annotations
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING
if TYPE_CHECKING:
    from myapp.models.user import User

sections.title: 3. __pycache__ and Bytecode Caching
sections.description:
Python compiles source to .pyc bytecode files stored in __pycache__. The .pyc file's hash matches the source's hash to detect staleness.

-B flag: skip bytecode generation. PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1: same effect. Useful for read-only filesystems or packaging.

Bytecode is NOT machine code — it's still interpreted by the CPython VM. .pyc loading is ~50% faster than parsing source.

FAANG angle: 'How does .pyc invalidation work?' — CPython stores the source file's modification timestamp and size alongside the bytecode. If the source is newer, the .pyc is regenerated. Use -B for ephemeral containers.
sections.codeBlock:
# Python bytecode compilation
# Source: hello.py
def greet(name):
    return f'hello {name}'

# After first import:
# __pycache__/hello.cpython-312.pyc

# Disassemble bytecode
import dis
dis.dis(greet)
# Output:
#   2           0 RESUME                   0
#   3           2 LOAD_CONST               1 ('hello ')
#               4 LOAD_FAST                0 (name)
#               6 FORMAT_VALUE             0
#               8 BUILD_STRING             2
#              10 RETURN_VALUE

# Skip .pyc generation
# $ python -B script.py
# $ PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1 python script.py

# Check .pyc location
from myapp.models import user
print(user.__pycache__)  # /path/to/myapp/models/__pycache__

sections.title: 4. Namespace Packages
sections.description:
Python 3.3+ allows namespace packages — packages split across multiple directories without __init__.py. Each directory with the same name contributes its modules to a single namespace package.

Use cases: (1) Splitting a large package across repos (e.g., google.cloud.storage and google.cloud.bigquery). (2) Plugin systems where each plugin is a directory with the same namespace. (3) Monorepo with independently-versioned subpackages.

Resolving conflict: If both a regular package (with __init__.py) and a namespace package have the same name, the regular package wins.

FAANG angle: 'Your monorepo has infra/metrics and infra/logging in different directories. How do you import both as infra.metrics and infra.logging?' — namespace package: create infra/ directories in each location without __init__.py.
sections.codeBlock:
# Standard regular package (with __init__.py)
# infra/metrics/__init__.py
# infra/metrics/gauge.py
# import infra.metrics.gauge  # works

# Namespace package (without __init__.py)
# /team-a/infra/logging/
#   └── logger.py
# /team-b/infra/metrics/
#   └── gauge.py

# Both PYTHONPATH entries resolve under 'infra' namespace
# import infra.logging.logger
# import infra.metrics.gauge

# Regular package coexists with namespace
# If both exist, regular package wins (has __init__.py)

# Creating namespace packages via pkg_resources
from pkgutil import extend_path
__path__ = extend_path(__path__, __name__)  # legacy approach,
# modern Python 3.3+ just omits __init__.py

sections.title: 5. Lazy Loading and Large Package Architecture
sections.description:
Lazy loading defers importing heavy modules until needed. Reduces startup time. Implement via __getattr__ on the module (PEP 562) or importlib.util.LazyLoader.

Submodule vs top-level: Import from the module that owns the symbol, not its parent. from pandas import DataFrame (not from pandas.core.frame import DataFrame).

Monorepo package strategy: Use setup.py with namespace packages for independently-deployable components. Each component has its own setup.py.

FAANG Staff+ angle: 'Design the package structure for a machine learning training framework' — namespace packages for core/trainer/data/eval modules, lazy loading for heavy dependencies (tensorflow/pytorch), and __all__ exports at the top-level.
sections.codeBlock:
# === lazy loading via module __getattr__ (PEP 562) ===
# heavy/__init__.py
from typing import Any

def __getattr__(name: str) -> Any:
    if name == 'ExpensiveModel':
        from .model import ExpensiveModel
        return ExpensiveModel
    raise AttributeError(f'module has no attribute {name}')

# Client code:
# from heavy import ExpensiveModel  # triggers lazy import

# === importlib lazy loader ===
from importlib.util import LazyLoader
import importlib

spec = importlib.util.find_spec('pandas')
pandas = importlib.util.module_from_spec(spec)
spec.loader.exec_module(pandas)  # normal eager load

# Lazy alternative (rare):
spec.loader = LazyLoader(spec.loader)
spec.loader.exec_module(pandas)  # defers sub-imports

# === Large package organizing ===
# myml/
#   __init__.py        # imports Model, train, evaluate
#   model/
#     __init__.py      # exports BaseModel
#     layers/
#     losses/
#   data/
#     __init__.py
#     dataset.py
#     transforms.py
#   train/
#     trainer.py
#     config.py
#   evaluate/
#     metrics.py

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• Python Import System — Official reference
  • importlib module — Import machinery implementation
  • PEP 420 — Implicit Namespace Packages
  • PEP 562 — Module __getattr__ and __dir__

details:
Import workflow: (1) Use absolute imports by default. (2) Prefer python -m package.module over direct python module.py. (3) Use __init__.py to aggregate clean public APIs. (4) Use TYPE_CHECKING to break circular import cycles. (5) Use namespace packages for cross-repo package splitting.

Common mistakes: Running python module.py inside a package (breaks relative imports); circular imports from putting too much in __init__.py; modifying sys.path at runtime; not specifying __all__; using from package import * (pollutes namespace, hides dependencies).

Staff+ expectations: Design the package architecture for the team's monorepo. Use namespace packages for independently-versioned components. Implement lazy loading for startup-time optimization. Ensure clear import boundaries between modules to prevent circular dependencies.
